coredata.dc:title: Polymer dots of DASA-functionalized polyethyleneimine: Synthesis, visible light/pH responsiveness, and their applications as chemosensors
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Dan
Cao, Ziquan
Wu, Bo
Zhang, Qing
Wang, Guojie
coredata.dc:description: Visible light/pH dual-responsive polymer dots (PDs) were facilely prepared from branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) functionalized with donor-acceptor Stenhouse adducts (DASAs). The synthesized polymer dots were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, 1H NMR spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The emission peak of the DASA-PEI polymer dots changed as the excitation wavelength varied from 360nm to 520nm. The strongest fluorescence emission was located around 522nm upon 420nm excitation. Moreover, the DASA-PEI polymer dots exhibited a typical photo-responsive behavior. After visible light irradiation, the triene of DASAs isomerized to the cyclopentenone form. Interestingly, the DASA-PEI polymer dots also responded to acid and alkali and showed a reversible transition between the triene and cyclopentenone isomers. It is demonstrated that the prepared DASA-PEI polymer dots could be applied as a turn-off chemosensor for the rapid and selective detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in aqueous solutions with detection limits of 10.1nM and 1.3nM, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Nanotechnology
Polyethyleneimine
Polymer dot
Sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic Scheme for the Preparation of the DASA-PEI and Schematic Illustration of the DASA-PEI Polymer Dots under Visible Light, Acid (pH=6) and Base (pH=8) Stimulation.
XPS spectrum of the DASA-PEI polymer dots (a) and the high resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d) of the polymer dots.

(a) TEM image of the DASA-PEI polymer dots; (b) size distributions for the polymer dots in ethanol obtained from DLS analysis; (c) UV–vis absorption, excitation spectrum (Ex., λem =520nm) and emission spectrum (Em., λex =420nm) of the polymer dots in ethanol; (d) fluorescence spectra of the polymer dots at different λex from 320 to 520nm in ethanol.

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectra of the DASA-PEI polymer dots in ethanol upon visible light irradiation (520nm, 30mW/cm2) for 12min, at pH 6 and pH 8, respectively; (b) absorbance of the polymer dots at 522nm upon the cyclic switching under alternating conditions of pH 6 and pH 8; (c) photographs of the polymer dots in ethanol before and after visible light irradiation, or adding acid and base.
TEM images of the DASA-PEI polymer dots: (a) after visible light irradiation (520nm, 30mW/cm2, 12min); (b) at pH 8; and (c) at pH 6.

(a and b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the DASA-PEI polymer dots (0.1mgmL−1) in aqueous solution in the presence of different amount of Cu2+ (0, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100, and 200μM) and Fe3+ (0, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500μM) respectively; (c and d) the relationship between fluorescence emission intensities at 475nm and the concentration of Cu2+ and Fe3+ respectively; (e and f) Stern-Volmer plots as a function of Cu2+ and Fe3+ concentration respectively.

The difference in relative fluorescence intensities of the DASA-PEI polymer dots for the aqueous solutions containing different metal ions (excitation at 360nm, [Mn+]=500 μM, F and F0 are fluorescence intensities of PDs at 475nm in the presence and absence of metal ions, respectively).

head.ce:title: Polymer dots of DASA-functionalized polyethyleneimine: Synthesis, visible light/pH responsiveness, and their applications as chemosensors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing, 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Visible light/pH dual-responsive polymer dots (PDs) were facilely prepared from branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) functionalized with donor-acceptor Stenhouse adducts (DASAs). The synthesized polymer dots were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, 1H NMR spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The emission peak of the DASA-PEI polymer dots changed as the excitation wavelength varied from 360nm to 520nm. The strongest fluorescence emission was located around 522nm upon 420nm excitation. Moreover, the DASA-PEI polymer dots exhibited a typical photo-responsive behavior. After visible light irradiation, the triene of DASAs isomerized to the cyclopentenone form. Interestingly, the DASA-PEI polymer dots also responded to acid and alkali and showed a reversible transition between the triene and cyclopentenone isomers. It is demonstrated that the prepared DASA-PEI polymer dots could be applied as a turn-off chemosensor for the rapid and selective detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in aqueous solutions with detection limits of 10.1nM and 1.3nM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer dots (PDs) are a kind of fluorescent nanoparticles, which possess sub-fluorophores (which are groups such as CO, CN, NO) instead of typical conjugated fluorophore groups and have a great potential as novel fluorescent materials [1,2]. The strong fluorescence of polymer dots could afford the system as efficient bio-fluorophores for targeted cell imaging [3]. The multifunctional PDs possessing bright and multicolor fluorescence with high drug loading capacity could be used for imaging-guided drug delivery [4]. The polymer dots could be also used as fluorescent chemical sensors to sensitively and selectively detect metal ions [5,6]. Recently, the stimuli-responsive fluorescent materials have attracted ever-increasing attention for their wide applications in the construction of novel detection, imaging, biolabeling, and optoelectronic systems [7]. Of particular relevance to the polymer dots, various research groups have exploited pH, thermal and redox effects on carbon dots [8–13], which have been used in bioimaging [8–12], drug delivery [8,9], and chemosensors [13]. However, to the best of our knowledge, the dual-responsive polymer dots have not been reported to date.

It is well known that visible light is the most widely available, non-invasive, and environmentally benign external stimulus with the light advantages of spatial and temporal resolution [14–16]. Donor-acceptor Stenhouse adducts (DASAs) are a new class of visible light responsive materials, which can switch from a conjugated, colored, and hydrophobic form to a ring-closed, colorless, and zwitterionic structure upon irradiation with visible light [17–21]. The visible light responsive micelles prepared from DASA-functionalized amphiphilic polymers could display on-demand light-mediated disassembly and cargo release [17]. DASA grafted on amino functionalized polycarbonate surfaces exhibited excellent lithographic performance using visible light, where well-defined patterns could be replicated with high precision and resolution by using suitable masks [19]. A polymeric probe derived from the visible light responsive DASA was designed for the rapid and selective colorimetric detection of nerve agent mimics [21]. As mentioned above, numerous works have been reported concerning polymer dots and visible light responsive DASA-functionalized polymers, however, less attention has been given to the polymer dots based on DASAs. Herein, we report a new kind of DASA-functionalized polymer dots prepared from polyethyleneimine (PEI) and 5-(furan-2-ylmethylene)-2, 2-dimethyl-1, 3-dioxane-4, 6-dione (FYD) shown in Scheme 1 , which exhibited a characteristic excitation wavelength dependent emission and visible light responsive characteristics. Interestingly, the DASA-PEI polymer dots also showed pH responsiveness. The fluorescence of DASA-PEI PDs could be effectively and selectively quenched by Fe3+ and Cu2+ in aqueous solutions. We believe that this discovery of the DASA-functionalized polymer dots offers considerable scope for the design of dual-stimuli responsive polymer dots and their applications in detecting Fe3+ and Cu2+.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw=600), 2-furfural and Meldrum’s acid were obtained from Aladdin reagent. Other chemicals were obtained from Beijing Chemical Corp (Beijing, China). Laboratory grade solvents like ethanol and THF were used without any pre-treatment. Deionized water with a conductance below 10−6 Scm−1 was applied during the experiments. Stock solutions of metal cations were prepared in distilled water from the respective salts of FeCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, CaCl2, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, AgNO3, MgSO4, NaCl, KCl, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, MnSO4·H2O, and Cr(NO3)3·9H2O.

The compound FYD was synthesized according to the literature.17 2, 2-Dimethyl-1, 3-dioxane-4,6-dione (1.51g, 10.5mmol) and 2-furaldehyde (960mg, 10mmol) were mixed in water (30mL) and stirred at 75°C for 2h. After cooling to room temperature, a yellow precipitate was formed. After purification by washing, the compound FYD, a bright yellow powder, was obtained (2.1g).

PEI (0.1g) and 5-(furan-2-ylmethylene)-2, 2-dimethyl-1, 3-dioxane-4, 6-dione (0.1g) were dissolved in ethanol (5mL). Then the system was stirred at room temperature for 15min and the color of the mixture changed from yellow to pink. The resultant PDs were precipitated by adding excess THF, which were refined by dissolving in ethanol and precipitating into excess THF 3 times to remove the unreacted FYD. Finally, a pink powder (0.13g) was obtained after filtering and drying in a vacuum oven for 24h.

Quantum yield (QY) measurements were performed according to the established procedure. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (quantum yield 0.54) was used as a standard [22]. In order to minimize re-absorption effects, the absorbance of the solutions for PDs and quinine sulfate was kept below 0.05 at 360nm. The QY was calculated using the below equation:
ϕ X
=
ϕ
S T
(
I X
/
I
S T
) (
A
S T
/
A
A X
) (
η X
2
/
η
S T
2
)
Where ϕ is the QY, I is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent; ST denotes the standard and X denotes the sample.

The metal ion solutions were prepared from FeCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, CaCl2, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, AgNO3, MgSO4, NaCl, KCl, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, MnSO4·H2O, and Cr(NO3)3·9H2O in distilled water with a concentration of 500 μM. For metal ion detection, a calculated amount of Fe3+ and Cu2+ (0, 10, 20, 30,50,100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 μM) was added to 1mL aqueous solutions containing 100 μg PDs, and allowed to react for 3min under gently shaking. Then, fluorescence spectra were recorded. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

1H NMR spectroscopy was conducted with a Bruker AM 400 spectrometer with DMSO-d 6 as the solvent. The UV–vis spectra of the samples were recorded using a UV–vis spectrometer (JASCO V-570). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Specifically, samples were placed in a 10mm optical path length quartz fluorescence cuvette. The emission slit and the exciting slit were both 5nm. The morphologies of the polymer dots were characterized using a JEM-2010 EX/S Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM). The TEM samples were prepared by a solution (10 μL, 3mgmL−1) on 300-mesh copper grids, where excess solution was wicked away with filter paper. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were carried out on the Zetasizer Nano ZS90 instrument (Malvern Instruments) equipped with a multi-222 purpose autotitrator (MPT-2) as the light source. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer spectrophotometer at 25°C in the range between 4000 and 500cm−1. The polymers were thoroughly mixed with KBr and pressed into a flaky form before FTIR experiments. The polymer molecular weight was detected by gel permeation chromatography (GPC, Waters 1515) with styragel columns relative to polystyrene standards. Nanosecond fluorescence lifetime experiments were performed by the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system under right-angle sample geometry. An Edinburgh EPL 375nm picosecond diode laser with a repetition rate of 40kHz was used to excite the samples. The fluorescence was collected by a photomultiplier tube (Hamamatsu R928p) connected to a Becker & Hickl SPC-130TCSPC board. The time constant of the instrument response function was 100ps. For the visible light irradiation, the DASA-PEI solutions (about 2mL of the aqueous solution in cuvettes) were placed vertically under a LED irradiator (wavelength: 520nm, intensity: 35mW/cm2). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed by using a Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi electron spectrometer.

The DASA-PEI polymer dots were synthesized by grafting PEI with 5-(furan-2-ylmethylene)-2, 2-dimethyl-1, 3-dioxane-4, 6-dione (FYD) in ethanol at room temperature (Scheme 1). The reactants changed to the products facilely, where the color of the mixture turned from yellow to pink in several minutes. The compound FYD was synthesized by reacting 2-furfural with Meldrum’s acid[17]. The chemical structure and composition of DASA-PEI polymer dots was investigated by XPS, 1H NMR and FTIR. The XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 1 a reveals three prominent features of C1s at 285eV, O1s at 532eV and N1s at 399eV, with atomic percentages of 77.76%, 8.45% and 13.79%, respectively. The typical C1s spectrum exhibits four distinct peaks located at 284.2, 284.9, 285.8 and 288.5eV, which correspond to CC, CC, CN/CO and CO groups respectively, shown in Fig. 1b. The O1s spectrum can be resolved into two components at 531.1 and 532.3eV, associated with CO and COC/COH bands respectively, shown in Fig. 1c [23]. Deconvolution of the N1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) shows two components at 399.1 and 399.7eV, which are attributed to the N–(C)3, and NH/NH2 groups, respectively [24]. The 1H NMR spectrum of the DASA-PEI is shown in Fig. S1, where the peaks at 5.5-7.5ppm correspond to carbon–carbon double bond protons of DASA and the peaks at 2.6ppm correspond to the methylene protons of PEI [25]. The FTIR spectra of PEI and DASA-PEI PDs are presented in Fig. S2, from which it can be seen that several distinct characteristic peaks are observed for the DASA-PEI PDs. Remarkably, the peak centered at 1202cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO [26], and the stronger peak at 1600cm−1 is attributed to CX (X=C or O) stretching vibration of the PDs [27]. The GPC traces of the PDs and the reactant PEI are shown in Fig. S3, from which it can be seen that the average molecular weight of the PDs is higher (Mw=800, PDI=1.25).

Fig. 2 a shows the TEM image of the prepared DASA-PEI polymer dots from ethanol solution, from which it can be seen that the PDs were well-dispersed with a spherical size around 150nm. The hydrodynamic size of the PDs in ethanol solution was measured to be 342nm by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. 2b). It should be noticed that the DLS size is larger than the TEM diameter because dynamic light scattering (DLS) gives the size of solvent-swollen aggregates. Fig. 2c illustrates the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of the DASA-PEI in ethanol. Two characteristic UV–vis absorption peaks located at 366nm and 522nm were observed. The peak centered at 366nm is ascribed to the n–π* transition and the contribution of surface moieties of polymer dots [24]. The peak centered at 522nm is ascribed to conjugated olefinic functional groups due to the triene derivatives [20]. Upon excitation at the maximum excitation wavelength of 420nm, the fluorescence spectrum exhibits a strong peak at 520nm, shown from the excitation and emission spectra in Fig. 2c. As shown in Fig. 2d, DASA-PEI PDs exhibited extraordinarily a characteristic excitation wavelength dependent emission. With increasing the excitation wavelength from 360 to 520nm, the emission peak red-shifts from 465 to 565nm. Unlike the significant red-shift of PDs, PEI showed negligible fluorescence when excited at wavelengths above 420nm and thus resulted in no obvious visible green fluorescence [5]. It should be noted that the DASA and the precursor FYD did not possess the excitation-dependent fluorescence either, shown in Fig. S4a and S5b . Thus, it can be concluded that the excitation-dependent emission of the DASA-PEI was not resulted from PEI, DASAs and precursors FYD. The excitation-dependent emission properties are probably attributed to the complexity of surface excited states of the PDs [28,29].

The DASA-PEI polymer dots in ethanol solution could exhibit a typical photo-responsive behavior, shown in Fig. 3 a. After visible light irradiation, the absorption band at 522nm diminished, indicating that the triene units isomerized to the cyclopentenones. When adjusting the DASA-PEI ethanol solution to alkaline (pH=8), the absorption band centered at 522nm also decreased, indicating that the isomerization from triene to cyclopentenone occurred. When adjusting the DASA-PEI alkaline solution to acidic (pH=6), the absorption band centered at 522nm could be recovered, indicating that the isomerization from cyclopentenone to triene occurred. The pH-switched Z-E isomerization of the DASA-PEI polymer dots could also involve a proton-transfer process [20]. Fig. 3b shows that the absorbance of the polymer dots at 522nm changed alternately upon switching between pH 6 and pH 8, indicating that the polymer dots possessed a good pH-switched reversibility. Fig. 3c shows the color change upon the stimulation. The primary DASA-PEI PDs solution was pink, the color could change to yellow when irradiated with visible light or adding base, and then the pink would be recovered when adding acid. The fluorescence emission spectra of the PDs in ethanol after visible light irradiation, at pH 6 and pH 8 show that the PDs still possess a characteristic excitation-dependent emission after the stimulation, as depicted in Fig. S6a, c and e. Compared with that of the primary PDs, the fluorescence could increase by one third after visible light irradiation or at basic conditions, while the fluorescence decreased at acidic conditions. The fluorescence changes upon the stimulation can be associated to the corresponding aggregation changes, as will be discussed later in the text. For comparison, the fluorescence emission of the DASAs in ethanol were also measured after visible light irradiation (520nm, 30mW/cm2), at pH 6 and pH 8, shown in Fig. S6b, d and f, from which it can be seen that there was no shift of emission when varying the excitation wavelength and the fluorescence intensity changed little upon the stimulation.

Besides the above absorbance and fluorescence changes upon light and pH stimulation, the stimuli-responsive morphologies of the PDs were also investigated. Fig. 4 shows the TEM images of the PDs in ethanol after visible light and pH stimulation. After visible light irradiation, the size of the DASA-PEI PDs decreased to ∼70nm shown in Fig. 4a; at pH 8, the size of nanoparticles declined to ∼6nm shown in Fig. 4b. The isomerization of the hydrophobic triene to hydrophilic cyclopentenone upon the stimulation would induce the nanoparticles to be dissociated and reduce the particle size [30–32]. Thus the fluorescence of the polymer dots would increase after the stimulation shown in Fig. S6a and e, since small aggregates could suffer less fluorescence quenching. Fig. 4c shows that the size of nanoparticles declined to ∼60nm at pH 6. Under acidic conditions, PEI possesses considerable positive charges, leading to an expansion of PEI chains because of the repulsion between the charged amine groups. The charge–charge repulsions may be minimized by forcing the charged moieties as far apart as possible [33]. It is noted that some DASA-PEI polymer dots could precipitate out of the ethanol solution at the acidic condition, which would decrease the fluorescence of the polymer solution shown in Fig. S6b. The size distribution of the PDs in ethanol after the stimulation was determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) shown in Fig. S7. The aggregation changes determined by DLS are in accordance with those revealed by TEM.

It is noted that the isomerization from triene to cyclopentenone is very fast in the presence of water [20]. Once the polymer dots were dissolved in water, the pink color could turn into yellow in several seconds. The TEM image of the DASA-PEI polymer dots in aqueous solution is shown in Fig. S8a, which shows the average diameter was about 65nm. The hydrodynamic size of the PDs in aqueous solution was measured to be 200nm by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. S8b). The DASA-PEI PDs in aqueous solution also exhibited a characteristic excitation wavelength dependent emission. With increasing the excitation wavelength from 360 to 520nm, the emission peak red-shifted from 465 to 565nm. The strongest fluorescence emission is located at around 475nm upon 360nm excitation, shown in Fig. S8c. The quantum yield (QY) of the as-prepared PDs in aqueous solution at room temperature was found to be 2.1% using quinine sulfate as a reference. Cu2+ and Fe3+ are well-known to be high toxic to organisms and the detection of such metals is thus highly desired [34–36]. Here we explored the PDs for the detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous solutions. The photoluminescence emission spectra of the polymer dots mixed with various concentrations of Cu2+ or Fe3+ were recorded. As depicted in Fig. 5 a and b, the fluorescence of the polymer dots decreased significantly with the increase of the concentration of the metal ions. The relationships between fluorescence emission intensities at 475nm and the concentration of Cu2+ and Fe3+ are shown in Fig. 5c and d, respectively. Fig. 5e reveals a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F-1) and Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0–50μM (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of PDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively). The detection limit (LOD) for Cu2+ is as low as 1.3nM based on 3σ/k (σ represents the standard deviation for the corrected blank signals with 20 parallel measurements, and k is the slope of the calibration curve), which is much lower than the safe limit of Cu2+ ions in water (20μM) set by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [37]. Fig. 5f reveals the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F-1) and Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0–50μM. The detection limit (LOD) for Fe3+ is 10.1nM, which could compare favorably with those previous reports for Fe3+ detection [24,26,38].

Fig. 6 shows the screening experiments with different metal ions (500μM) by investigating the fluorescence intensity changes of the PDs. The fluorescence intensities of the PDs were greatly decreased by the addition of Fe3+ and Cu2+, while the other ions showed less or no effect, indicating the prepared PDs could be used as promising fluorescence probes for selective detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+. The high selectivity of PDs for Fe3+ and Cu2+ may be due to the fact that the Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions have a higher binding affinity and faster chelating kinetics with N and O functional groups of PDs than other transition-metal ions, facilitating aggregation of the PDs and induced fluorescence quenching as indicated previously [5,39–41]. Large PDs-Cu2+ and PDs-Fe3+ aggregates were indeed observed in our work, shown in Fig. S10. The fluorescence decay dynamics was measured to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the system. As shown in Fig. S11, the fluorescence lifetime of PDs was measured to be 1.63ns. After coordination with Cu2+ ions, the lifetime decreased to 1.30ns. The decrease of the lifetime indicates an ultrafast PDs/Cu2+ electron-transfer process, resulting in the dynamic quenching. Besides, the fluorescence excitation spectra of the PDs (0.1mgmL−1) in the presence of different metal ions were examined (Fig. S13). The excitation intensity of PDs decreased dramatically in the presence of Cu2+ (200μM) or Fe3+ (500μM), accompanied with an obvious red-shift. The above results clearly demonstrate that the PDs showed good selectivity toward Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions.

In summary, we have facilely synthesized the visible light- and pH-responsive polymer dots from the branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) functionalized with donor-acceptor Stenhouse adducts. The visible light and pH triggered properties and morphological changes of the DASA-PEI polymer dots are revealed by UV−vis spectra, fluorescence spectra, transmission electron microscopy and dynamic light scattering. The prepared DASA-PEI PDs exhibited a characteristic excitation wavelength dependent emission and the strongest fluorescence emission appeared around 520nm upon 420nm excitation in ethanol solution. After visible light irradiation, the triene of DASA-PEI PDs isomerized to cyclopentenone and the color changed from pink to yellow correspondingly. Moreover, the prepared PDs could also exhibit pH-responsive behaviors. At alkaline condition, the triene of DASA-PEI PDs could also isomerize to cyclopentenone. At acidic condition, the triene could be recovered from cyclopentenone. The prepared DASA-PEI showed a good pH-switched reversibility of the isomerization and the color change. It is demonstrated that the DASA-PEI PDs could be applied as the fluorescent chemosensors for detection of metal ions with high sensitivity and selectivity, i.e. the detection limits for Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions could reach as low as 10.1nM and 1.3nM, respectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51373025 and 21074010), Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 2112029) and the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-11-0582).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.07.107.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary materials for this article: 1H NMR spectra, FT-IR spectra, DLS, fluorescence spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra, and TEM images.
The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Chem. Commun.

Macromol. Rapid Commun.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dan Zhong received his B.S. degree from Beijing University of Chemical Technology in 2014. She then joined Prof. Guo-Jie Wang’s group at the University of Science and Technology Beijing and received his M.S. degree in 2017. Her research was focused on photoresponsive polymer materials.

Zi-Quan Cao received his B.S. degree from Nanchang Hangkong University in 2012. He then joined Prof. Guo-Jie Wang’s group at the University of Science and Technology Beijing as a Ph.D. candidate. His research is focused on the photoresponsive polymer materials.

Bo Wu received his B.S. degree from Shaanxi University of Science & Technology in 2014. He then joined Prof. Guo-Jie Wang’s group at the University of Science and Technology Beijing as a Ph.D. candidate. His research is focused on fluorescence-based biosensors.

Qing Zhang received his B.S. degree from Anhui University of Science & Technology in 2014. He then joined Prof. Guo-Jie Wang’s group at the University of Science and Technology Beijing and received his M.S. degree in 2017. His research was focused on fluorescent polymer dots.

Guo-Jie Wang is currently a professor at the University of Science and Technology Beijing. He received his Ph.D. in Chemistry from Jilin University in 2000. He worked as a postdoctoral fellow at Tsinghua University from 2000 to 2002. He then joined the Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, as an associate professor. In 2005, he moved to the School of Chemistry and Biochemistry at the Georgia Institute of Technology (GIT) as a research fellow. After his one-year stay at GIT, he moved to the Department of Chemistry at the Katholieke Universiteit Leuven. In 2008, he joined the School of Materials Science and Engineering at the University of Science and Technology Beijing as a full professor. Prof. Wang’s research interests include photoresponsive materials, polymeric nanomaterials for controlled release and fluorescence-based bio/chemical sensors.
